verses.text: Thus all the work that Solomon made for the house of the LORD was finished: and Solomon brought in all the things that David his father had dedicated; and the silver, and the gold, and all the instruments, put he among the treasures of the house of God. Then Solomon assembled the elders of Israel, and all the heads of the tribes, the chief of the fathers of the children of Israel, unto Jerusalem, to bring up the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of the city of David, which is Zion. Wherefore all the men of Israel assembled themselves unto the king in the feast which was in the seventh month. And all the elders of Israel came; and the Levites took up the ark. And they brought up the ark, and the tabernacle of the congregation, and all the holy vessels that were in the tabernacle, these did the priests and the Levites bring up. Also king Solomon, and all the congregation of Israel that were assembled unto him before the ark, sacrificed sheep and oxen, which could not be told nor numbered for multitude. And the priests brought in the ark of the covenant of the LORD unto his place, to the oracle of the house, into the most holy place, even under the wings of the cherubims: For the cherubims spread forth their wings over the place of the ark, and the cherubims covered the ark and the staves thereof above. And they drew out the staves of the ark, that the ends of the staves were seen from the ark before the oracle; but they were not seen without. And there it is unto this day. There was nothing in the ark save the two tables which Moses put therein at Horeb, when the LORD made a covenant with the children of Israel, when they came out of Egypt. Guidance: The work is finished, and now comes its crowning moment: the ark of the covenant is brought up to its place in the most holy place, beneath the outstretched wings of the cherubim. All Israel assembles, the priests and Levites bear the ark (now rightly, on their shoulders), and innumerable sacrifices are offered. The chapter is showing us the culmination of all David's longing and all Solomon's labour — the ark of God, the symbol of His covenant presence, brought home at last to rest in the house built for His name. The chapter is teaching us that the goal of all the building, all the gold, all the labour, was to make a place for the presence of God among His people; the temple without the ark would have been an empty shell. So in our lives and churches, all our building and ordering is in vain unless it makes room for the living presence of God. The chapter is teaching us, in the careful note that 'there was nothing in the ark save the two tables' of the law given at Horeb, that the word of God — His covenant law — lay at the very heart of His dwelling. The presence of God and the word of God belong together; at the center of the most holy place was the law, beneath the mercy seat where atonement was made. This teaches us that God's word stands at the heart of true religion, and that His holy law is met by His covering mercy. The chapter is teaching us, in the wings of the cherubim spread over the ark, the gracious presence of God enthroned above the place of atonement, His glory attended by the angelic host. The chapter is teaching us that when we have done all our work for God, the one thing needful is His presence; let us labour not merely to build, but to make room for the LORD Himself to dwell among us, and to set His word at the very center of all we are.

The chapter is teaching us, in the careful note that 'there was nothing in the ark save the two tables' of the law given at Horeb, that the word of God — His covenant law — lay at the very heart of His dwelling. The presence of God and the word of God belong together; at the center of the most holy place was the law, beneath the mercy seat where atonement was made. This teaches us that God's word stands at the heart of true religion, and that His holy law is met by His covering mercy. The chapter is teaching us, in the wings of the cherubim spread over the ark, the gracious presence of God enthroned above the place of atonement, His glory attended by the angelic host. The chapter is teaching us that when we have done all our work for God, the one thing needful is His presence; let us labour not merely to build, but to make room for the LORD Himself to dwell among us, and to set His word at the very center of all we are.

And it came to pass, when the priests were come out of the holy place: (for all the priests that were present were sanctified, and did not then wait by course: Also the Levites which were the singers, all of them of Asaph, of Heman, of Jeduthun, with their sons and their brethren, being arrayed in white linen, having cymbals and psalteries and harps, stood at the east end of the altar, and with them an hundred and twenty priests sounding with trumpets:) It came even to pass, as the trumpeters and singers were as one, to make one sound to be heard in praising and thanking the LORD; and when they lifted up their voice with the trumpets and cymbals and instruments of musick, and praised the LORD, saying, For he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever: that then the house was filled with a cloud, even the house of the LORD; So that the priests could not stand to minister by reason of the cloud: for the glory of the LORD had filled the house of God. Guidance: Here is the glorious climax. The singers and trumpeters — Asaph, Heman, Jeduthun, arrayed in white linen, with a hundred twenty priests — lifted up their voices and instruments 'as one, to make one sound,' praising the LORD with the great refrain, 'For he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever.' And in that moment of united, wholehearted praise, 'the house was filled with a cloud... for the glory of the LORD had filled the house of God,' so that the priests could not even stand to minister. The chapter is showing us the descent of God's glory in response to the united praise of His people; when they worshiped Him with one heart and one voice, exalting His goodness and everlasting mercy, He came down and filled the house with His presence. The chapter is teaching us the power and beauty of unity in worship: 'the trumpeters and singers were as one, to make one sound.' Where God's people praise Him with one heart, undivided and harmonious, there His presence is manifested. Division and discord grieve the Spirit; oneness in praise invites the glory of God. The chapter is teaching us the theme of true worship — 'For he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever' — the everlasting goodness and mercy of God, the song that filled heaven and earth and drew down His glory. The chapter is teaching us that the goal and crown of all worship is the manifest presence of God; the priests could not stand for the cloud, for the LORD Himself had taken possession of His house. This is what all the building was for — not the gold or the architecture, but that the glory of God might fill the place. The chapter is teaching us to seek, above all, the presence and glory of God in our worship; to praise Him with one heart for His goodness and unfailing mercy; and to long that His glory would so fill our gatherings, our hearts, and our lives that all else is eclipsed by the overwhelming sense of His presence. When God's people are one in praising His goodness and mercy, the glory comes down — and that glory is the whole point and prize of worship.
